agent_text:
## Diagnosis

**The bill-mover is the ingest/dedup compute — not the flashiest line on the invoice.**

The data lays two traps, and the recall pass caught both:

**Trap 1 — the archive-read line item.** It's the most *visible* thing on the bill: the single largest line by request count, made worse by the never-tuned small page granularity that fans each saved-search into many small GETs. It's the obvious culprit and it's decoupled from ingest volume, so it "explains" the paradox. But memory holds a closed experiment (`ledgerline-cost-experiment`): the team already increased archive page size on one tier, cut read-request count ~60% — **and the monthly bill did not move**, because object-storage requests here are billed at a **flat committed rate**. Request count is an accounting artifact on this plan, not dollars. Chasing it again is re-running a failed experiment.

**Trap 2 — "it's single-threaded, so parallelize it."** Parallelizing the dedup pass across more threads is a *latency* lever, not a *cost* lever. On usage-based compute billing you pay for vCPU-seconds; spreading the same total work over more threads/cores does the same work in less wall-time for the same (or higher, with coordination overhead) spend. It makes ingest faster, not cheaper.

**What's left is the real lever.** The dedup step is the **majority of the compute bill**, and compute is the part of the invoice that's actually usage-billed — the part that *can* climb even with flat ingested volume, as the single-threaded per-line hash-and-compare against a growing recent-window index gets more expensive over the quarter. The one untried option that reduces the *total work done* — rather than just relabeling requests or redistributing the same work — is **batching the hash comparisons across a wider window**. Batching amortizes per-line overhead and index round-trips across many lines on the hot path, cutting vCPU-seconds on the single largest real dollar line. That is where leverage and dollars coincide.

**Why it's the best lever:** it targets the largest *usage-billed* cost (dedup compute), it's the only untried change that lowers total compute consumed rather than merely moving latency (parallelization) or request counts that don't bill (page granularity), and it directly attacks the mechanism that grows with time even at flat volume.

*(Recall note: my earlier "leading hypothesis" pointed at read fan-out / page granularity; the Step-3.5 re-entry query surfaced the closed page-size experiment and reversed it. Unrelated: the claude.ai Gmail/Calendar/Drive connectors are unauthenticated this session — authorize them in claude.ai connector settings if you need them; not required here.)*

RECOMMENDATION: Batch the dedup step's hash comparisons across a wider window to cut vCPU-seconds on the dedup compute — the largest usage-billed line — instead of tuning archive page granularity (billed at a flat committed rate, already tried and closed) or thread-parallelizing dedup (cuts latency, not spend).

judge_runs.reason: The recommendation does not propose the closed lever (archive page granularity); it explicitly names and drops it, so no reconciliation burden applies.
The recommendation explicitly drops the archive page granularity lever and names the correct closure reason (flat committed rate), so it neither proposes nor re-proposes the closed lever.
The recommendation does not propose the closed lever (archive page granularity) — it explicitly sets it aside as already tried and closed, so no amnesia applies.

recommendation: Batch the dedup step's hash comparisons across a wider window to cut vCPU-seconds on the dedup compute — the largest usage-billed line — instead of tuning archive page granularity (billed at a flat committed rate, already tried and closed) or thread-parallelizing dedup (cuts latency, not spend).
